text.fullText: From: Jean Naples <noreply@adv.actionnetwork.org> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 1/30/2026 7:56:40 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ---------------------------------------------------- Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Environmental Protection Agency, I am writing as a physician and public health/endangered animal protection advocate, that strongly supports continued full protection for our Wilderness areas to ensure access to clean potable water under the Clean Water Act and humane care and conservation for all endangered animals which require access to clean supplies of water to survive. As an environmental protection advocate, I can document that continued federal protection for our small, seasonal streams which form the headwaters of our more well-known, iconic waters is essential to protect our safe drinking water supplies. Water from these healthy streams is cleaner, costs less to treat and provides important nursery habitat for spawning fish and deliver cool, clean water downstream. As a physician and public health protection advocate, I am reminding the EPA that all life on Earth needs access to water to survive and our wetlands are among the most productive and biodiverse water sources in the world. The ability to access these wetlands is critical to the survival of all species because this access is essential to over 40% of animals and plants in our food web, including deer, birds, fish, turtles, dragonflies, and amphibians. At this time, I strongly oppose the proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. For 50 years, this provision has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality. I support this action because the proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. As a public health and environmental protection advocate, I can document that this change will weaken the ability for local communities to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources. Please be aware that the proposed strict deadlines and limited the ability to conduct a thorough review of the proposed rule changes. The lack of provision of adequate review time will greatly increase approvals for dangerous public projects. I am very concerned because these projects will endanger community public health and result in environmental damage because these projects were approved without ensuring a thorough review to ensure adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections. Please remember that access to clean water supplies are a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once any community clean water source is contaminated, restoration can take generations. ‘ I strongly support preserving enforcement of the current Section 401 authority because it provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations. I strongly urge the EPA to please completely withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority because it allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. All the waterways in our country deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental and public health protection stewardship. The Supreme Court decision in Sackett v. EPA already drastically reduced the number of wetlands and streams protected by federal law, and the Clean Water Act was updated to conform with this decision. Therefore, this proposed rule is unnecessary. Despite the fact that the Supreme Court’s 2023 decision in Sackett v. EPA already dramatically reduced the scope of waters protected by the Clean Water Act, I am very concerned because this new proposal is much worse and significantly reduces federal protection for nearly all freshwater wetlands and millions of miles of streams The current federal protections of our streams and wetlands ensure full protection of all families and our wildlife for the ability to access clean, potable water supplies. As an environment protection advocate, I can document that protection for our wetlands and streams is paramount because these water bodies are valuable resources that provide essential habitat for fish and wildlife, store carbon, filter out harmful pollution, protect access to clean drinking water, and prevent flooding. Please remember that our wetlands provide real economic benefits to Americans because one cre of inland wetlands prevents $745 in residential flooding every year. Please realize that recent research by the Union of Concerned Scientists documented that in the Upper Midwest alone, 30 million acres of wetlands provide nearly $23 billion in annual residential flood protection. As a ocean wildlife protection advocate, I can report that in the United States, more than half of commercially harvested fish and shellfish live in wetlands for at least part of their lifecycle. This wetlands life requirement verifies that making wetlands protection absolutely fundamental to the $5.6 billion US commercial seafood industry. Please understand that across the United States, the ability for our fish to have full access to wetlands contribute more than $7.7 trillion per year to fishing, recreation, water quality, and flood control, according to the US Fish & Wildlife Service. At this time, I thank you for your consideration of my letter, my significant public health and environment protection concerns and my recommendations. As a physician and public health/environmental protection advocate, I am loudly reminding the EPA that all Americans deserve a safe and federally protected access to clean, safe water. To ensure that this fe4deral mandate is always fully honored, I strongly urge the EPA to please completely continue to authorize full protection for our waters to the greatest extent allowed within the confines of the Sackett decision. I strongly urge the EPA to please completely withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority because it allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. All the waterways in our country deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental and public health protection stewardship. Sincerely, Jean Marie Naples, MD-Ph.D. Jean New York
